quotes.verbatim_quote:
Counterpoint is, in reality, the movement of two or more melodies rather than the
use of chord harmony. It plays a very important part in modern music even though it ts
from the "Old School". Some small combos derive great benefit from the use of it.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Foundational definition of counterpoint as melodic movement rather than chordal harmony.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
When two lines move against one another that is a form of Counterpoint. There are times
when one melody sustains itself for as many as 4 beats, while the other line moves along
in quarter or eighth notes.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Operative rule for recognizing counterpoint and introduces sustained vs. moving line rhythmic contrast.
Defines the two technical terms labeling each voice.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
Most of the examples in this book are brief and move chordally from one direction to an-
other. Though they never linger on one note for any length of time, there is never a sign
or sound of bad harmonic movement.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Articulates the book's governing aesthetic rule — avoid bad harmonic movement regardless of melodic independence.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
Exercise 65 illustrates a melody and counter melody moving along simultaneously. This
is the same example in exercise 49 of lesson 19. The difference is that only the first note
in each chord of the counter melody is used. This ts a sustaining type of counter melody
and it offers a strong straight line quality while the melody dances along on top of it.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Describes the technique of extracting only the first note from each chord to create a sustained bass-line counter melody.
